coredata.dc:title: Fluorescent responsive membrane based on terbium coordination polymer and carbon dots with AIE effect for rapid and visual detection of fluoroquinolone
coredata.dc:creator: Hu, Yunyun
Chen, Xi
Wang, Kai
Jiang, Chuang
Liu, Wenya
Zhang, Siyu
Zheng, Mingming
Zhou, Yibin
Xiao, Yaqing
Liu, Yingnan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect and antenna effect, a novel portable fluorescent responsive membrane was constructed with red carbon dots (R-CDs) as reference signal and terbium coordination polymer (Tb-AMP CPs) as response signal for visual, instrument-free, and sensitive detection of fluoroquinolones (FQs). Specifically, the fluorescent responsive membrane (R-T membrane) was prepared by physically depositing R-CDs with AIE property and Tb-AMP CPs on the surface of polyvinylidene fluoride filter membranes at ambient temperature. In the presence of FQs, Tb3+ in the Tb-AMP CPs of the prepared membrane coordinated with the β-diketone structure of FQs, which turned on the yellow-green fluorescence through the “antenna effect”. As the concentration of FQs increased, the R-T membrane achieved a fluorescent color transition from bright pink to yellow-green. Its visual detection sensitivity for three FQs, including ciprofloxacin, difloxacin, and enrofloxacin, was 0.01 μM, and the detection limits were 7.4 nM, 7.8 nM, and 9.2 nM, respectively, by analyzing the color parameter green. In the residue analysis of FQs in real samples, the constructed membrane also exhibited remarkable anti-interference and reliability, which is of great significance for ensuring the safety of animal-derived food.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Fluoroquinolone
Fluorescent responsive membrane
Antenna effect
Aggregation induced emission
Visual analysis

xocs:item-toc-entry.xocs:item-toc-section-title: Fabrication of the fluorescent responsive membrane and the detection of FQs
Analysis of FQs in real samples
Fabrication of fluorescent responsive membrane and its detection mechanism
Detection of FQs in real samples

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China
xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency: National Natural Science Foundation of China

xocs:funding.xocs:funding-agency-matched-string: Anhui Agricultural University Foundation for Stability and Introduction of Talent

xocs:funding-list.xocs:funding-text: Tb-AMP CPs was prepared by self-assembly of Tb3+ with AMP (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c), and their microstructure was characterized by TEM images. As shown in Fig. 1A and B, AMP was in a state of aggregation and cross-linking, and after coordination with Tb3+ its morphology transformed into uniform and dispersed spherical structure. The average particle size of the Tb-AMP CPs was 0.2 ± 0.07 μm (n = 131). The hydrodynamic diameter of the Tb-AMP CPs was evaluated by dynamic light scattering (DLS). AMP had a particle size of 259.9 nm (Fig. S1A), while Tb-AMP CPs had a particle size of 361.3 nm (Fig. S1B). The increase in particle size reflected the crosslinking between Tb3+ and AMP. Additionally, it was worth mentioning that the hydrodynamic diameter was significantly larger than that the particle size measured in TEM image. This may be due to the fact that the hydrodynamic diameter included an extended molecular layer of the material, while the particles in the TEM image were in a dry state (Ku and Kulkarni, 2018). The crystal structure of the Tb-AMP CPs was analyzed by XRD. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, compared with AMP, the crystallinity of Tb-AMP CPs was significantly reduced and there was no obvious characteristic peak in the range of 5°–90°, which indicated that the Tb-AMP CPs belong to the amorphous structure (Liu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022a, b, c). This amorphous structure was usually the result of irregular polymerization during synthesis (Boone et al., 2024). These data provided a strong support for the successful synthesis of the Tb-AMP CPs.Furthermore, Fig. 2F presents that the presence of Tb-AMP CPs attenuates the fluorescence lifetime of FQs from 1.32 ns to 0.63 ns. It indicated that the quenching mechanism of Tb-AMP CPs on FQs was dynamic quenching in which the energy was transferred rapidly through non-radiative pathways due to molecular diffusion encounter (Lai et al., 2023). This provided a strong support for the occurrence of antenna effect. The corresponding energy transfer mechanism can be further elucidated in Fig. 2G. Specifically, the luminescence Tb-AMP CPs was sensitized by FQs through the “antenna effect” produced via the coordination of Tb3+ with the β-diketone structure of FQs. As a ligand with strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, FQs can transition from the ground state (S0) to the first excited singlet (S1) during excitation, and then to the first excited triplet (T1) by intersystem crossing (ISC). The energy transfer from the 5D4 level to the 7Fj (j = 3～6) level caused Tb3+ to exhibit characteristic peaks. And emitted green fluorescence. This green fluorescence triggered by Tb3+ was enhanced with the increase of FQs concentration (Zhou et al., 2023). In addition, according to Reinhold's rule of thumb (Steemers et al., 1995), the energy gap (ΔETD) between the ligand's triplet state (T1) and the excited state of the lanthanide ion should be greater than 3500 cm−1 for the lanthanide ion to be irreversibly sensitized (Boone et al., 2024). As previously reported (Singha and Ahn, 2016), the T1 of FQs was 25,037 cm−1 and the ΔETD between the excited state level of Tb3+ (20,500 cm−1) was 4537 cm−1. This indicated that the energy transfer of FQs to Tb3+ was fast and irreversible (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c).The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China (32202159) and the Anhui Agricultural University Foundation for Stability and Introduction of Talent (rc352109). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XRD analysis.

The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China ( 32202159 ) and the Anhui Agricultural University Foundation for Stability and Introduction of Talent ( rc352109 ). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab ( www.shiyanjia.com ) for the assistance of XRD analysis.

ce:caption.ce:simple-para: Characterization of Tb-AMP CPs and R-CDs. (A, B) TEM images of (A) AMP and (B) Tb-AMP CPs and the corresponding particle size distribution. (C) XRD patterns. (D) FT-IR spectra of AMP and Tb-AMP CPs. (E) XPS survey spectra. The inset showed Tb 3d high-resolution XPS spectra of Tb-AMP CPs. (F, G) N 1s high-resolution XPS spectra of (F) AMP and (G) Tb-AMP CPs. (H) HR-TEM image and the corresponding particle size distribution. The inset displayed photo of the R-CDs powder under daylight. (I) XPS survey spectrum of R-CDs. (J–L) High-resolution XPS spectra of C 1s, S 2p, and N 1s. (M–O) Photos of R-CDs ethanol aqueous solutions under (M) 365 nm UV lamp, (N) daylight, and (O) 254 nm UV lamp. The water content ranged from 0% to 90%.

(A) Fluorescence emission spectra of FQs, Tb-AMP CPs, and Tb-AMP CPs/FQs solutions at 360 nm excitation wavelength. The inset was the corresponding fluorescent photo of the solutions under 365 nm UV lamp. (B) Fluorescence emission spectra of Tb-AMP CPs solutions with different concentrations of FQs. (C) Fluorescence photos of Tb-AMP CPs suspension (a) and Tb-AMP CPs/FQs suspension (b) before and after centrifugation under 365 nm UV lamp. (D) FT-IR spectra of FQs and Tb-AMP CPs/FQs. (E) UV–vis absorption spectra of FQs, Tb-AMP CPs and Tb-AMP CPs/FQs solutions. The inset was the corresponding photo of the solutions under daylight. (F) Fluorescence lifetime decay curves. (G) Diagram of the “antenna effect” and energy transfer between levels of “antenna effect”. (H) Photos of the blank membrane, R-CDs modified membrane, Tb-AMP CPs modified membrane, R-CDs and Tb-AMP CPs modified membrane (R-T membrane) under daylight and 254 nm UV lamp. (I) SEM images of the four membranes. (J) Photos of the four membranes before and after reaction with FQs solution of 5 μM under 254 nm UV lamp.

Optimization of detection conditions. (A, B) Fluorescence images of the R-T membranes modified with different concentrations of Tb-AMP CPs before and after reaction with FQs solution (5 μM) and the corresponding G value graph of coffee rings. Reaction time: 20 min. (C, D) Fluorescence images of the R-T membranes before and after reaction with FQs solution (5 μM) of different pH and the corresponding G value graph of coffee rings. Concentration of Tb-AMP CPs in the R-T membrane: 2.5 mg/mL. Reaction time: 20 min. (E, F) The fluorescence images of the R-T membranes after reaction with FQs solution for different time and the corresponding G value graph of coffee rings. Concentration of Tb-AMP CPs in the R-T membrane: 2.5 mg/mL. The results obtained were expressed as average ± standard deviation from at least three repeats.

Analytical performance evaluation of the developed R-T membrane. (A, B) The response sensitivity of the R-T membrane to CIP solution and the corresponding relationship curves between G value and CIP concentration. (C, D) The response sensitivity of the R-T membrane to DIF solution and the corresponding relationship curves between G value and DIF concentration. (E, F) The response sensitivity of the R-T membrane to ENR solution and the corresponding relationship curves between G value and ENR concentration. (G) The response sensitivities of the Tb-AMP CPs membrane and the blank membrane to CIP solution under 254 nm UV lamp. (H) The photograph of the fluorescence response of the R-T membrane to interfering substance and the corresponding G value diagram. (I) The photograph of the fluorescence response of the R-T membrane when the interfering substance coexisted with FQs and the corresponding G value diagram. The results obtained were expressed as average ± standard deviation from at least three repeats.
Schematic diagram of the R-T membrane construction and its use to detect FQs.
Comparison of the analytical performances with other methods for the detection of FQs.

head.ce:title: Fluorescent responsive membrane based on terbium coordination polymer and carbon dots with AIE effect for rapid and visual detection of fluoroquinolone

ce:affiliation.ce:textfn: Key Laboratory of Jianghuai Agricultural Product Fine Processing and Resource Utilization of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, College of Tea & Food Science and Technology, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China

sa:affiliation.sa:organization: Key Laboratory of Jianghuai Agricultural Product Fine Processing and Resource Utilization of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs
College of Tea & Food Science and Technology

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Key Laboratory of Jianghuai Agricultural Product Fine Processing and Resource Utilization of Ministry of Agriculture and Rural Affairs, College of Tea & Food Science and Technology, Anhui Agricultural University, Hefei, 230036, China

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect and antenna effect, a novel portable fluorescent responsive membrane was constructed with red carbon dots (R-CDs) as reference signal and terbium coordination polymer (Tb-AMP CPs) as response signal for visual, instrument-free, and sensitive detection of fluoroquinolones (FQs). Specifically, the fluorescent responsive membrane (R-T membrane) was prepared by physically depositing R-CDs with AIE property and Tb-AMP CPs on the surface of polyvinylidene fluoride filter membranes at ambient temperature. In the presence of FQs, Tb3+ in the Tb-AMP CPs of the prepared membrane coordinated with the β-diketone structure of FQs, which turned on the yellow-green fluorescence through the “antenna effect”. As the concentration of FQs increased, the R-T membrane achieved a fluorescent color transition from bright pink to yellow-green. Its visual detection sensitivity for three FQs, including ciprofloxacin, difloxacin, and enrofloxacin, was 0.01 μM, and the detection limits were 7.4 nM, 7.8 nM, and 9.2 nM, respectively, by analyzing the color parameter green. In the residue analysis of FQs in real samples, the constructed membrane also exhibited remarkable anti-interference and reliability, which is of great significance for ensuring the safety of animal-derived food.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Fluoroquinolones (FQs) are a class of chemically synthesized antibiotics that are used in the poultry industry to treat and prevent bacterial infections by destroying the DNA helicase and topoisomerase IV of bacteria (Kurrey et al., 2019). However, due to irrational use and poor biodegradability, they easily remain in food animals and further transfer to the human body through the food chain. This can cause various diseases such as diarrhea, typhoid fever, lower respiratory tract infections, skin, bone, and joint infections. Therefore, it is necessary to develop an effective and accurate method for FQs residue detection. At present, different analytical approaches have been established for FQs detection, including liquid chromatography (Czyrski and Sznura, 2019; Patyra and Kwiatek, 2017), gas chromatography (Li et al., 2011), mass spectrometry and their combinations (Wang et al., 2021). Despite the excellent sensitivity and reliability of these methods, their wide application is limited by expensive instruments, reliance on specialized technicians, and complex sample preparation (Liu et al., 2023).

In order to overcome these shortcomings, enzyme-linked immunoassay (Yuan et al., 2020), electrochemical analysis (Abnous et al., 2017; Rudnicki et al., 2020), capillary electrophoresis (Fan et al., 2009), fluorescence analysis (Jiang et al., 2023; Liu et al., 2022; Tong et al., 2023), and other rapid detection methods have been developed. Among them, fluorescence analysis has the advantages of simple equipment, low cost, convenient operation, rapid response, high sensitivity, and easy visualization, which is widely used in the detection of FQs (Gao et al., 2022; Korah et al., 2022). For example, Ouyang et al. used Fe3+ as the recognition molecule and the quencher to construct a fluorescence nanosensor based on upconversion nanoparticles for FQs detection (Ouyang et al., 2023). Kundu et al. used the hydrogen bonding between the carboxyl group of rice husk-derived carbon dots prepared and the amine in the piperazine ring of FQs to achieve a highly selective and sensitive detection of FQs (Kundu et al., 2022). However, most of the reported fluorescent probes rely on the enhancement or attenuation of a single signal to indicate FQs concentration. They detection performance is easily disturbed by environmental factors (such as pH and polarity of the medium) and lamp bleaching, and the color change is not easy to be recognized by the naked eye (Zeng et al., 2021). The introduction of reference signal can not only improve the sensitivity and accuracy through self-calibration, but also facilitate visual monitoring. However, to the best of our knowledge, reports on the use of dual-signal fluorescence probes to detect FQs are extremely limited.

Lanthanide coordination polymers (Ln-CPs) is a new type of multifunctional materials formed via the self-assembly of lanthanide ions and organic bridging ligands (Spokoyny et al., 2009). Due to abundant surficial active sites and excellent optical properties, they have attracted extensive attention in the fields of chemical analysis, catalysis and biomedical imaging (Liu et al., 2016a,b; Yin and Tong, 2022; Zeng et al., 2017, 2021). In various Ln-CPs, terbium coordination polymer is used for the detection of FQs owing to that it can be chelated with β-diketone moiety in FQs and thereby sensitized luminescence through the “antenna effect” (Zhou et al., 2023). For instance, Xu et al. prepared a coordination polymer nanosheets composed of adenosine 5′-monophosphate (AMP) and Tb3+ for the determination of FQs in milk samples (Xu et al., 2019). In addition, Ma et al. constructed a multicolor luminescent sensor that recognized FQs using the coordination polymer formed by guanosine monophosphate and Tb3+ as the host matrix and the copper nanoclusters formed by glutathione and Cu2+ as the guest species (Ma et al., 2023). Although encouraging results were obtained, the detection capability of these fluorescent probes is limited by the solution state. Furthermore, fluorescence quenching caused by aggregation of high concentration fluorophores limits the overall brightness of probe solution, which is not conducive to visual recognition (Chai et al., 2021; Severi et al., 2022).

Motivated by these facts, we designed a novel, simple, and portable fluorescent responsive membrane for sensitive, visual, and instrument-free detection of FQs based on aggregation-induced emission (AIE) effect and antenna effect. As shown in Scheme 1 , terbium coordination polymer (Tb-AMP CPs) formed via the self-assembly of Tb3+ and AMP at room temperature and hydrophobic red carbon dots (R-CDs) prepared by solvothermal reaction were modified on the surface of polyvinylidene fluoride filter membrane via physical deposition. Among them, Tb-AMP CPs acted as the response signal and R-CDs served as the reference signal. Compared with traditional fluorophores, R-CDs with AIE effect overcame the fluorescence quenching of traditional fluorophore in high concentration or aggregation state, and provided a strong reference fluorescence for visual fluorescence analysis. On the other hand, the few functional groups on the surface also prevent R-CDs from interacting with FQs and Tb-AMP CPs, thus reducing interference and improving detection accuracy. When the FQs solution was added to the surface of the membrane, Tb3+ in Tb-AMP CPs coordinated with the β-diketone structure of FQs, which turned on the yellow-green fluorescence of the R-T membrane through the “antenna effect”. As the concentration of FQs increased, the R-T membrane achieved a fluorescent color transition from bright pink to yellow-green. Therefore, a simple, highly selective, and visualized fluorescence-responsive membrane was developed, and its practical application potential was demonstrated by its application to the detection of FQs residues in egg samples. This work provides a reference for visual analysis of food safety hazard factors.

Melamine (MA), dithiosalicylic acid (DTSA), 2-[4-(2-hydroxyethyl)-1-piperazinyl] ethane sulfonic acid (HEPES), adenosine 5′-monophosphate (AMP) were all obtained from the Aladdin Chemical Reagent Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Terbium nitrate hexahydrate (Tb(NO3)3‧6H2O), sodium hydroxide (NaOH), ciprofloxacin (CIP), difloxacin (DIF), enrofloxacin (ENR), gentamycin (GEN), chloramphenicol (CHL), roxithromycin (ROX), lincomycin (LIN), streptomycin (STR), kanamycin (KAN), nitrofurazone (NIT), ampicillin sodium (AMP), tetracycline (TC), and azithromycin (AZI) were provided by Macklin Biochemical Technology Co., Ltd. (Shanghai, China). Analytical grade reagents (NaCl, KCl, CaCl2, MgCl2, ZnCl2, AlCl3, Na2SO4, Na2SO3 Na2CO3, and Na2HPO4) were purchased from Sinopharm Chemical Reagent Company (Shanghai, China). Polyvinylidene fluoride filter membrane (0.22 μm) was supplied by Xinya Purification Device Factory (Shanghai, China).

Scanning electron microscopy (SEM, S-4800 Hitachi, Japan), transmission electron microscopy (TEM, HT-7700 Hitachi, Japan), and high-resolution transmission electron microscopy (HR-TEM, FEI Tecnai G2 F30, USA) were used for micromorphology analysis. The hydrodynamic diameter and zeta potential were determined using the Zeta potential analyzer (Zeta, ZSU 3100, UK). The crystal structure was revealed by X-ray diffractometer (XRD, SmartLab SE, Japan). X-ray photoelectron spectroscopy (XPS, Thermo Fisher Scientific, USA) and Fourier transform infrared spectroscopy (FT-IR, Nicolet 6700, USA) were used to analyze chemical structure and elemental composition. Fluorescence spectrophotometer (PE-LS55, PerkinElmer, USA) and ultraviolet–visible (UV–vis) spectrophotometer (UV-2600i, Shimadzu, Japan) were used to analyze the optical properties.

Tb-AMP CPs was prepared using the self-assembly method (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c). Briefly, 2 mL of Tb(NO3)3·6H2O solution (10 mM) and 2 mL of AMP solution (10 mM) were added to 6 mL HEPES solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4). After continuous stirring for 2 h, the resulting mixed solution was centrifuged at 10,000 rpm (4 °C) for 10 min, and the white precipitate was washed with water to remove the unreacted reagents. Tb-AMP CPs were obtained after 24 h freeze-drying, and they were dispersed in HEPES solution (0.1 M, pH = 7.4) for the following experiments.

R-CDs with AIE effect was synthesized according to Yang's report (Yang et al., 2019). MA (201.5 mg) and DTSA (544 mg) were dissolved in 40 mL of acetic acid. The resulting solution was transferred to a 100 mL high-pressure reactor and heated at 180 °C for 10 h. Subsequently, the mixture was poured into boiling water and filtered to remove excess reagent. The precipitate was vacuum-dried at 60 °C to obtain R-CDs powder.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of the fluorescent responsive membrane and the detection of FQs
ce:section.ce:para: R-CDs ethanol solution (0.125 mg/mL, 4 mL) were uniformly mixed with Tb-AMP CPs HEPES solution (2.5 mg/mL, 4 mL). The mixture was deposited on the surface of the filter membrane by vacuum filtration. The obtained membrane was dried at ambient temperature and named as the R-T membrane. Besides, the R-CDs membrane was prepared with HEPES solution instead of Tb-AMP CPs solution, and the Tb-AMP CPs membrane was obtained by replacing the R-CDs ethanol solution with ethanol solution. As for the blank membrane, it was prepared by replacing Tb-AMP CPs HEPES solution with HEPES solution and R-CDs ethanol solution with ethanol solution, respectively. For FQs detection, 15 μL of FQs solution was dripped onto the R-T membrane. After 20 min, the fluorescence color change of the R-T membrane was recorded by a camera under 254 nm UV lamp.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Analysis of FQs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: Eggs purchased from a local supermarket were selected as real samples to investigate the practical application potential of the R-T membrane constructed by a standard addition assay. The samples were pretreated according to the previously proposed method prior to detection (Ribeiro et al., 2016; Zhou et al., 2023). Briefly, known concentrations FQs solutions were added to 0.50 g homogenized egg sample. Subsequently, 4.0 mL acetonitrile solution containing 1% acetic acid was added and the mixed solution was ultrasonic treated for 20 min. It was centrifuged (10,000 rpm, 5 min) to remove the denatured protein precipitate, and the collected supernatant was transferred to a beaker. After the solution was diluted eight times, its pH was adjusted to neutral with 0.1 M NaOH solution. The obtained extract was used for FQs analysis using the prepared R-T membrane according to the procedure of 2.5.

Tb-AMP CPs was prepared by self-assembly of Tb3+ with AMP (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c), and their microstructure was characterized by TEM images. As shown in Fig. 1 A and B, AMP was in a state of aggregation and cross-linking, and after coordination with Tb3+ its morphology transformed into uniform and dispersed spherical structure. The average particle size of the Tb-AMP CPs was 0.2 ± 0.07 μm (n = 131). The hydrodynamic diameter of the Tb-AMP CPs was evaluated by dynamic light scattering (DLS). AMP had a particle size of 259.9 nm (Fig. S1A), while Tb-AMP CPs had a particle size of 361.3 nm (Fig. S1B). The increase in particle size reflected the crosslinking between Tb3+ and AMP. Additionally, it was worth mentioning that the hydrodynamic diameter was significantly larger than that the particle size measured in TEM image. This may be due to the fact that the hydrodynamic diameter included an extended molecular layer of the material, while the particles in the TEM image were in a dry state (Ku and Kulkarni, 2018). The crystal structure of the Tb-AMP CPs was analyzed by XRD. As illustrated in Fig. 1C, compared with AMP, the crystallinity of Tb-AMP CPs was significantly reduced and there was no obvious characteristic peak in the range of 5°–90°, which indicated that the Tb-AMP CPs belong to the amorphous structure (Liu et al., 2020; Wang et al., 2022a, b, c). This amorphous structure was usually the result of irregular polymerization during synthesis (Boone et al., 2024). These data provided a strong support for the successful synthesis of the Tb-AMP CPs.

Furthermore, the chemical structure and elemental composition of the Tb-AMP CPs were investigated by FT-IR and XPS spectra. As shown in Fig. 1D, the AMP spectrum exhibited characteristic peaks at 1506 cm−1, 1036 cm−1, and 988 cm−1, which were assigned to stretching of the N7–C8 (νN7-C8) bond in the adenine subunit, antisymmetric phosphate vibrations (νasPO3), and symmetric phosphate vibrations (νsPO3), respectively (Huang et al., 2015). However, in the Tb-AMP CPs spectrum, these peaks were shifted to 1482 cm−1, 1111 cm−1, and 998 cm−1, respectively. According to previous reports (Nishiyabu et al., 2009; Tan et al., 2012; Wang et al., 2023), this may be due to the adenine portion and phosphate groups of AMP involved in the coordination with Tb3+. Fig. 1E shows the XPS survey spectra of the Tb-AMP CPs and AMP. It can be seen from the figure that the Tb-AMP CPs mainly contained C, N, O, P, and Tb elements, whose contents were 55.72%, 14.04%, 25.07%, 3.42% and 1.75%, respectively. The presence of the Tb element validated the combination of AMP and Tb3+. The inset in Fig. 1E presents the high-resolution pattern of Tb 3d, which exhibited characteristic peaks belonging to Tb 3d3/2 and Tb 3d5/2 at 1276.3 eV and 1241.9 eV (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c). The N 1s spectra of AMP (Fig. 1F) and the Tb-AMP CPs (Fig. 1G) were deconvolved into two peaks corresponding to imine N (400.33 eV) and pyridine N (399.08 eV, 399.58 eV) groups, respectively. Compared with AMP, N 1s (399.58 eV) in Tb-AMP CPs exhibited a higher binding energy, which may be due to the formation of Tb–N coordination bonds. O 1s (530.90 eV) in Tb-AMP CPs also demonstrated a similar phenomenon (Figs. S1C and S1D), possibly due to the presence of Tb–O coordination bonds (Zhou et al., 2023).

In addition, the optical properties of the Tb-AMP CPs were characterized by fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra. As can be seen from Fig. S1E, Tb-AMP CPs did not emit fluorescence compared with Tb3+. As described in many studies (Huang et al., 2015), this may be due to the quenching effect of water molecules occupying the remaining electron orbitals except AMP in Tb3+ coordination sphere. Fig. S1F demonstrates that AMP exhibits the maximum absorption peak derived from the nucleobase at 258 nm. After Tb3+ was adulterated, the absorption peak intensity significantly decreased and the peak position redshifted to 260 nm. This may be due to the coordination of AMP to Tb3+ by the nucleobase portion, which was consistent with previous findings (Liu et al., 2016a,b; Wang et al., 2023). The inset in Fig. S1F exhibits that AMP solution is clear and transparent with excellent solubility, while Tb-AMP CPs solution is insoluble in water and forms a suspension, showing an obvious Tyndall phenomenon. This may be due to the chemical coordination of the hydrophilic groups (nucleobases and phosphate groups) in AMP with Tb3+ (Xu et al., 2019). This phenomenon further proved that both the nucleobase part and the phosphate group in AMP were involved in the coordination. The above data together verified that the Tb-AMP CPs was successfully prepared.

CDs have unique optical properties due to their small size and surface structure, including high luminescent quantum yield (QY), remarkable biocompatibility, low toxicity, resistance to photobleaching, excellent photostability and dispersion properties (Zulfajri et al., 2020). These unique optical properties endow CDs surprising potential for various applications for in vivo and in vitro biosensing, bioimaging, and other applications (Ji et al., 2020; Hussain et al., 2023). However, traditional CDs are prone to fluorescence quenching due to aggregation at high concentrations, which limits the overall brightness of the system. CDs with AIE effect can overcome this phenomenon. In this study, R-CDs with AIE characteristic were prepared by solvothermal method using MA and DTSA as precursors, and the powder photo was shown in Fig. 1H. (Sun et al., 2021; Liu et al., 2022). The micromorphology was characterized by HR-TEM. As shown in Fig. 1H, R-CDs had a uniform monodisperse spherical structure with a particle size ranging from 1.04 nm to 3.01 nm. The average particle size was 2.2 ± 0.40 nm (n = 153). The chemical structure of R-CDs was characterized by FT-IR and XPS spectra. As illustrated in Fig. S2A, the surface of R-CDs included S–S (491 cm−1), C–S (687 cm−1), C–O (1142 cm−1), aromatic C–NH (1251 cm−1), CC (1453 cm−1), amide carbonyl (1674 cm−1), CN (2056 cm−1), S–H (2651 cm−1), and methylene (2853 cm−1) groups (He et al., 2017; Wang et al., 2017; Yang et al., 2019). However, the hydrophilic hydroxyl (3092 cm−1) and amino (3422 cm−1) derived from DTSA and MA significantly decrease on the surface of R-CDs, possibly due to the occurrence of amidation and carbonization reactions (Liu et al., 2022). This result reflected the hydrophobicity of R-CDs. The XPS spectra in Fig. 1I presented four peaks at 398.95 eV, 163.13 eV, 531.86 eV, and 284.08 eV, indicating that R-CDs was composed of N (1.22%), S (9.26%), O (18.95%), and C (70.57%) elements. The high-resolution spectrum of C 1s was deconvolved into two peaks (Fig. 1J), which were attributed to CO/CN (287.58 eV) and C–C/CC (284.08 eV), respectively (Yang et al., 2019). The high-resolution spectrum of S 2p displayed three peaks corresponding to the S–C (162.68 eV), S–H (163.18 eV), and S–S (164.08 eV) (Fig. 1K) (Yang et al., 2020). The N 1s high-resolution spectrum in Fig. 1L presented two peaks derived from the pyridine C3–N and pyrrolic C2–N–H groups at 398.68 eV and 399.48 eV (Wang et al., 2019; Zhao et al., 2020). The above data indicated that S atoms and N atoms were successfully embedded in R-CDs, which corresponded to the FT-IR data.

The fluorescence emission spectra and UV–vis absorption spectra of R-CDs ethanol aqueous solutions with varying water contents were recorded to further reveal the optical properties of R-CDs. R-CDs solution with a water volume ratio of less than 50% displayed a blue fluorescence peak at 445 nm, and the peak intensity gradually decreased with the increase of water volume ratio (Fig. S2B). This may be due to the accumulation of hydrophobic R-CDs leading to the π-π stacking of carbonized nuclei. When the water volume ratio exceeded 50%, a new red fluorescence emission peak was observed at 618 nm. It may be attributed to the restriction of intramolecular rotation due to the aggregation of R-CDs, which activated the AIE effect (Liu et al., 2022; Yang et al., 2019). The intensity ratio of the two emission peaks (I618/I445) was positively correlated with the percentage water content. The fluorescence photo under 365 nm UV lamp in Fig. 1M further verified this phenomenon. The UV–vis absorption spectra were displayed in Fig. S2C. R-CDs ethanol aqueous solutions with varying water contents exhibited characteristic peaks at 311 nm, which was mainly caused by the π-π* transition of CC in the R-CDs core. The peak intensity gradually decreased with the increase of water content, indicating that the characteristic peak was related to the dispersion of R-CDs. Fig. 1N displays the corresponding photo of the solutions in daylight. The ethanol aqueous solution of R-CDs with low water content (less than 50%) appeared uniformly transparent pink. When the water content exceeded 50%, R-CDs accumulated and the solutions became cloudy. This provided strong support for the aggregation of hydrophobic R-CDs and the fluorescence color transformation caused by them. The fluorescence behavior of the solutions under 254 nm UV lamps was also investigated. The clarified R-CDs solutions with low moisture content had no fluorescence emission, while the R-CDs suspensions with high moisture content emitted pink fluorescence (Fig. 1O).

In addition, in order to further verify the AIE behavior of R-CDs, the DLS, HR-TEM, and zeta potential of R-CDs in ethanol and aqueous solutions were characterized. As shown in Fig. S3A, the hydrodynamic diameters of R-CDs in ethanol and aqueous solutions were 2.3 nm and 48.9 nm, respectively. The increase of particle size indicated that R-CDs formed aggregates in aqueous solution. HR-TEM images revealed that R-CDs dispersed in ethanol showed a uniformly dispersed spherical structure (Fig. 1H), while R-CDs dispersed in aqueous solution displayed a clustered cross-linked state (Fig. S3B). The corresponding mean particle size varied from 2.2 ± 0.4 nm (n = 153) (Fig. 1H) to 48.5 ± 8.9 nm (n = 23) (Fig. S3C). This further confirmed that R-CDs formed aggregates in aqueous solution. Fig. S3D presents that the zeta potentials of the R-CDs in aqueous solution and ethanol solution are 6.93 mV and 51.70 mV, respectively. The high potential indicated that R-CDs has excellent dispersibility and stability in ethanol solution, but tended to aggregate in aqueous solution. Therefore, the above data confirmed AIE effect of R-CDs.

Compared with the fluorescence behavior of the solutions under 365 nm UV lamp, the solutions under 254 nm UV lamp exhibited a lower fluorescence background, which was beneficial for acting as a reference signal for fluorescence analysis.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Fabrication of fluorescent responsive membrane and its detection mechanism
ce:section.ce:para: Taking CIP as a typical FQs, the interaction between FQs and Tb-AMP CPs was discussed. As shown in Fig. 2 A, FQs solution displayed a characteristic emission peak at 450 nm at 360 nm excitation wavelength, while the Tb-AMP CPs solution had only an extremely weak fluorescence peak at 545 nm. According to previous reports (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c; Ouyang et al., 2023), this may be due to the unsaturated coordination of Tb3+, which caused its partial empty orbital to be occupied by water molecules. And the high frequency vibration of the water molecules led to energy loss, thereby reducing the luminous efficiency. However, after adding FQs, the Tb-AMP CPs solution displayed four characteristic peaks at 489 nm, 545 nm, 587 nm, and 621 nm and emitted significant green fluorescence under 365 nm UV lamp (the inset in Fig. 2A). Moreover, the intensity of these characteristic peaks showed an increasing trend with the increase of FQs concentration (Fig. 2B). This may be due to the presence of FQs triggering an electronic transition of Tb3+ in Tb-AMP CPs from 5D4 to 7Fj (j = 3∼6) levels, effectively sensitizing Tb3+ and enabling energy transfer (Liu et al., 2016a,b; Boone et al., 2024). It's worth noting that no characteristic emission peak of FQs was observed in Fig. 2B. Furthermore, we also investigated the fluorescence behavior of Tb-AMP CPs/FQs in different states (Fig. 2C). In the presence of FQs, significant green fluorescence was observed in Tb-AMP CPs suspension. However, after centrifugation, only the precipitate showed green fluorescence, while the supernatant showed no fluorescence emission. This suggested that FQs was not simply adsorbed on the outer surface of Tb-AMP CPs, but strongly coordinated with Tb3+.

Therefore, we speculated that FQs molecules replaced the coordination water molecules around Tb3+ in Tb-AMP CPs and were coordinated to the Tb3+ as antenna molecules. The emission of Tb-AMP CPs was effectively sensitized by the “antenna effect”. To verify this conjecture, FT-IR spectra, UV–vis absorption spectra, and fluorescence lifetime were further measured. It can be observed from Fig. 2D that the characteristic peaks of symmetric stretching vibration derived from COO- (1312 cm−1) and CO (1443 cm−1) of FQs shifted to 1340 cm−1 and 1471 cm−1 in the Tb-AMP CPs/FQs spectrum. This change reflected the chemical coordination of COO- and CO groups in FQs with Tb3+ in Tb-AMP CPs (Tan et al., 2013). As shown in Fig. 2E, two maximum absorption peaks of FQs occurred at 270 nm and 334 nm, respectively, which were attributed to the π-π* transition of CO in the COO- and CO groups in FQs (Jiang et al., 2024). However, when FQs co-existed with Tb-AMP CPs, the absorption peak at 270 nm shifted to 250 nm and the absorption peak at 334 nm shifted to 300 nm. This result further confirmed that the β-diketone structure of FQs bound to Tb-AMP CPs via coordination bonds (Aimé et al., 2010; Tan et al., 2013).

Furthermore, Fig. 2F presents that the presence of Tb-AMP CPs attenuates the fluorescence lifetime of FQs from 1.32 ns to 0.63 ns. It indicated that the quenching mechanism of Tb-AMP CPs on FQs was dynamic quenching in which the energy was transferred rapidly through non-radiative pathways due to molecular diffusion encounter (Lai et al., 2023). This provided a strong support for the occurrence of antenna effect. The corresponding energy transfer mechanism can be further elucidated in Fig. 2G. Specifically, the luminescence Tb-AMP CPs was sensitized by FQs through the “antenna effect” produced via the coordination of Tb3+ with the β-diketone structure of FQs. As a ligand with strong absorption in the ultraviolet region, FQs can transition from the ground state (S0) to the first excited singlet (S1) during excitation, and then to the first excited triplet (T1) by intersystem crossing (ISC). The energy transfer from the 5D4 level to the 7Fj (j = 3～6) level caused Tb3+ to exhibit characteristic peaks. And emitted green fluorescence. This green fluorescence triggered by Tb3+ was enhanced with the increase of FQs concentration (Zhou et al., 2023). In addition, according to Reinhold's rule of thumb (Steemers et al., 1995), the energy gap (ΔETD) between the ligand's triplet state (T1) and the excited state of the lanthanide ion should be greater than 3500 cm−1 for the lanthanide ion to be irreversibly sensitized (Boone et al., 2024). As previously reported (Singha and Ahn, 2016), the T1 of FQs was 25,037 cm−1 and the ΔETD between the excited state level of Tb3+ (20,500 cm−1) was 4537 cm−1. This indicated that the energy transfer of FQs to Tb3+ was fast and irreversible (Wang et al., 2022a, b, c).

Thus, the FQs molecule can turn on the green fluorescence of Tb-AMP CPs through the “antenna effect”. Based on this, Tb-AMP CPs can serve as a recognition signal to construct a fluorescence probe for FQs detection. However, the change in a single fluorescence signal is difficult to recognize by the naked eye, which undoubtedly affects the accuracy of visual analysis. Moreover, at present, most of the fluorescent probes used for FQs detection are limited to the solution state, which is not conducive to real-time in-situ detection. Therefore, in this study, the hydrophobic R-CDs with AIE effect was introduced as the reference signal, and co-deposited with Tb-AMP CPs on the polyvinylidene fluoride filter membrane by physical deposition R-CDs were co-deposited to construct the fluorescence response membrane.

As shown in Fig. 2H, compared with the blank membrane, the Tb-AMP CPs membrane displayed no significant difference in both daylight and UV lamp. However, the SEM images in Fig. 2I presented that there were some densely distributed particles in the pores of the Tb-AMP CPs membrane, which provided strong evidence for the successful modification of Tb-AMP CPs. The R-CDs membrane appeared light pink in daylight and bright pink fluorescence in UV lamp, which verified the modification of R-CDs on the filter membrane. The SEM image of R-CDs membrane showed that there were some aggregate particles in the pores of the multi-layer porous membrane, which was caused by the aggregation of R-CDs due to the ethanol volatilization. This further verified that R-CDs was successfully deposited on the membrane surface. The R-T membrane exhibited colors similar to R-CDs membrane under daylight and UV lamp. The difference was that there were more dense particles in the pores of the R-T membrane, which verified the successful modification of Tb-AMP CPs and R-CDs.

The fluorescence responses of the constructed three fluorescent membranes to FQs solution was depicted in Fig. 2J. Only weak changes were observed in the fluorescence signal before and after the reaction of the blank membrane and R-CDs membrane with FQs which was due to the inherent fluorescence of FQs. This further indicated that neither the blank membrane nor R-CDs reacted with FQs and that R-CDs can act as a reference signal. After reacting with FQs the Tb-AMP CPs membrane exhibited a green fluorescent “coffee ring” through the “antenna effect”, while the R-T membrane displayed a yellow-green fluorescent “coffee ring”. In contrast, the fluorescent transition from bright pink to yellow-green was more easily recognized by the naked eye. Therefore, the developed R-T membrane was expected to be applied to the visual sensitive detection of FQs.

Experimental parameters including Tb-AMP CPs concentration, pH of the solution to be tested, and reaction time were crucial for the detection performance of the constructed R-T membrane. It can be seen from Fig. 3 A that the fluorescence of the prepared R-T membrane was not affected by the concentration of Tb-AMP CPs. However, after reaction with FQs solution, the fluorescence intensity of the yellow-green coffee rings on the R-T membrane gradually increased with the increase of Tb-AMP CPs concentration. When the concentration of Tb-AMP CPs was greater than 2.5 mg/mL, the fluorescence intensity of the coffee rings did not increase. The corresponding G value in Fig. 3B also confirmed this color change. This phenomenon may be attributed to that the saturation of the coordination between FQs molecules and Tb-AMP CPs, so that the antenna effect was no longer enhanced. Therefore, the optimum concentration of Tb-AMP CPs in the R-T membrane was set as 2.5 mg/mL.

Furthermore, the effect of pH of the solution to be tested on the detection performance of the R-T membrane was explored. As displayed in Fig. 3C, when the pH was 7.4, the fluorescence intensity of the coffee ring in the R-T membrane after reaction with CIP was the strongest. It can also be seen from the corresponding G value diagram that the fluorescence change of the R-T membrane was the most obvious at pH = 7.4 (Fig. 3D). Therefore, the pH of the solution to be tested was set to 7.4 for the subsequent experiments.

Additionally, the influence of the reaction time on the detection performance of the R-T membrane was investigated. As shown in Fig. 3E, the fluorescence intensity of yellow-green coffee ring was positively correlated with the reaction time, and no significant change was observed in the fluorescence intensity after 20 min. The G value in Fig. 3F also presented a corresponding trend of change. This reflected that the antenna effect reached dynamic balance within 20 min. On the basis, the optimal reaction time of the R-T membrane and FQs was set to 20 min. In addition, the duration of this test result was further investigated. As shown in Fig. S4, the fluorescence response of the R-T membrane to FQs remained stable and the corresponding G value did not change significantly in the subsequent 40 min (p > 0.05). This indicated that the prepared R-T membrane had excellent stability in the FQs detection process.

As a consequence, the optimal conditions for the R-T membrane detection of FQs were as follows: the concentration of Tb-AMP CPs was 2.5 mg/mL, the pH of the solution to be measured was 7.4, and the reaction time was 20 min.

With CIP, DIF, and ENR as the representatives of FQs, the sensitivity of the R-T membrane to FQs was evaluated by recording the fluorescence color changes caused by different concentrations of FQs solutions under the above optimal conditions and analyzing the corresponding G value. In Fig. 4 A, with the increase of CIP concentration, yellow-green coffee rings appeared on the bright pink R-T membrane and their fluorescence intensity gradually increased. The corresponding G values also gradually increased and showed a linear response to CIP concentration in the range of 0.01 μM–5 μM (Fig. 4B). The linear regression equation was y = 19.092x+157.627 (R2 = 0.994). According to the 3σ/slope principle (σ represents the blank standard deviation), the limit of detection (LOD) of the R-T membrane to CIP was calculated to be 7.4 nM. The R-T membrane also exhibited similar fluorescence responses to DIF and ENR (Fig. 4C and E), with linear regression equations y = 19.222x+144.764 (R2 = 0.994) (Fig. 4D) and y = 17.269x+141.326 (R2 = 0.990) (Fig. 4F), respectively. The LOD for DIF and ENR were 7.8 nM and 9.2 nM, respectively.

Furthermore, the fluorescence responses of the blank membrane and Tb-AMP CPs membrane to CIP were also investigated under the same conditions. As presented in Fig. 4G, only when the concentration of CIP reached 5 μM, the fluorescence change of the blank membrane can be observed by the naked eye. In contrast, the Tb-AMP CPs membrane presented a more sensitive color response to CIP. As the concentration of CIP increased, the fluorescence intensity of green coffee ring of the Tb-AMP CPs membrane gradually increased. Its visual LOD was 0.1 μM, which was much higher than that of the R-T membrane (0.01 μM). Besides, the single fluorescence intensity change of the Tb-AMP CPs membrane was not easily recognized by the naked eye.

The above data reflected that the constructed R-T membrane had a sensitive fluorescence response to FQs, and the fluorescence transition from red to yellow-green can be recognized by the naked eye without the assistance of precision instruments. Compared with other FQs detection methods (Table 1 ), the R-T membrane in this study displayed lower detection limits and a relatively wide detection range. It was expected to be applied to the sensitive, visual, and in-situ monitoring of FQs.

In view of the complexity of the actual sample, it is necessary to assess the selectivity and anti-interference ability of the prepared R-T membrane to FQs. As shown in Fig. 4H, only FQs (ENR, DIF, and CIP) caused significant coffee ring on the R-T membrane. Nevertheless, other interfering substances, including small molecules (glucose, cysteine, histidine), anions (CO3 2−, HPO4 −, SO4 2−, SO3 2−, AC−), cations (K+, Ca2+, Mg2+, Al3+, Zn2+), and other non-FQs antibiotics (azithromycin (AZI), tetracycline (TC), ampicillin sodium (AMP), nitrofurazone (NIT), kanamycin (KAN), streptomycin (STR), lincomycin (LIN), roxithromycin (ROX), chloramphenicol (CHL), and gentamycin (GEN)), had negligible effects on the fluorescence signal of the R-T membrane. This demonstrated that the constructed R-T membrane had excellent selectivity to FQs. Furthermore, the detection stability of the R-T membrane was investigated in the presence of other interfering substances and FQs. As shown in Fig. 4I, even if these interfering substances coexisted with FQs at concentrations of 10 times those of FQs, there was little interference with detection performance. Therefore, the developed portable membrane for FQs detection had excellent anti-interference performance.

ce:section.ce:section-title: Detection of FQs in real samples
ce:section.ce:para: The application potential of the prepared R-T membrane was confirmed by the detection of FQs residue in eggs via the standard addition assay. Egg samples were simply pretreated before being detected by adding an acetonitrile solution containing 1% acetic acid. This eliminated the protein in the egg from interfering with the detection signal. The extraction solution after protein removal was diluted to further minimize the influence of the interfering medium. The R-T membrane was used to analyze FQs residues in the tested solution according to the steps in Part 2.5. As revealed in Table S1, the recoveries of CIP, DIF, and ENR were 96.00%–111.60%, 96.00%–105.60%, and 99.20%–112.40%, respectively. The relative standard deviations (RSD) were lower than 3%, which reflected that the R-T membrane had remarkable stability in FQs detection. Compared with traditional methods that rely on large laboratory instruments, the detection membrane developed in this study was easy to operate and had a rapid response, which provided the possibility for field analysis.

In summary, this study constructed a straightforward and portable fluorescent responsive membrane for rapid, intuitive, and sensitive analysis of FQs on the basis of antenna effect. The membrane displayed excellent sensitivity and specificity to FQs through the coordination of Tb3+ in Tb-AMP CPs with the β-diketone structure of FQs. Under 254 nm UV lamp, yellow-green coffee rings gradually appeared on the surface of the bright pink membrane as the concentration of FQs increased. The visual detection limits of the membrane for CIP, DIF, and ENR were all 0.01 μM, and the detection limits calculated by analyzing the coffee ring's G value were 7.4 nM, 7.8 nM, and 9.2 nM, respectively. Compared with single-signal detection membrane, the introduction of reference signal improved the sensitivity and reliability of the detection by reducing the interference of environmental factors and increasing the contrast. More importantly, the developed membrane was successfully applied to the practical determination of FQs residues in egg samples with satisfactory recovery and accuracy. Overall, this study provides a new idea for the design of convenient, visual, in-situ, rapid screening methods for antibiotics, which has important value for strengthening the safety supervision of animal-derived foods. In addition, how to achieve accurate identification of multiple FQs in real samples is still a big problem in the field of rapid detection of FQs, and the future work will actively explore solutions.

Yunyun Hu: Writing – original draft. Xi Chen: Methodology. Kai Wang: Investigation. Chuang Jiang: Data curation. Wenya Liu: Software. Siyu Zhang: Validation. Mingming Zheng: Formal analysis. Yibin Zhou: Formal analysis. Yaqing Xiao: Conceptualization. Yingnan Liu: Funding acquisition.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: The authors gratefully acknowledge the financial supports of National Natural Science Youth Foundation of China (32202159) and the Anhui Agricultural University Foundation for Stability and Introduction of Talent (rc352109). The authors would like to thank the Shiyanjia Lab (www.shiyanjia.com) for the assistance of XRD analysis.
